title: Declaration of Independence
summary: The colonies declared independence and grounded the new polity in rights language and republican legitimacy claims.
destroyedOrDisplaced: imperial constitutional settlement
sourcesOrRationale: National Archives founding documents: Declaration of Independence history and related materials.

title: Constitution and Bill of Rights
summary: The Constitution and Bill of Rights created a more durable federal order while formalizing rights and the architecture of national power.
destroyedOrDisplaced: Articles of Confederation settlement
sourcesOrRationale: National Archives founding documents: Constitution and Bill of Rights.

title: Louisiana Purchase
summary: The Louisiana Purchase doubled the territorial scale of the United States and accelerated expansionist state ambition.
destroyedOrDisplaced: older territorial limits of the early republic
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica: Louisiana Purchase overview and significance.

title: Mexican-American War and Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo
summary: Expansion through war brought vast new territory while intensifying conflict over slavery, citizenship, and the moral terms of the republic.
destroyedOrDisplaced: any stable boundary between expansion and slavery politics
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica: Mexican-American War overview and outcomes.

title: Emancipation Proclamation
summary: Lincoln's proclamation transformed the Civil War into an explicit struggle over slavery and freedom.
destroyedOrDisplaced: the political fiction that the Union war was neutral on slavery
sourcesOrRationale: National Archives milestone documents listing and Emancipation Proclamation materials.

title: 14th Amendment
summary: The 14th Amendment redefined citizenship and equal protection, making federal civil rights claims central to the postwar republic.
destroyedOrDisplaced: prewar constitutional settlement over citizenship
sourcesOrRationale: National Archives: 14th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution.

title: Sherman Antitrust Act
summary: The federal government formally recognized monopoly power as a national political problem, even if enforcement remained uneven.
destroyedOrDisplaced: some assumptions that concentrated power was politically neutral
sourcesOrRationale: National Archives milestone documents coverage of antitrust-era federal regulation.

title: Plessy v. Ferguson
summary: The Supreme Court's 'separate but equal' ruling entrenched segregation and narrowed the practical reach of Reconstruction-era equality.
destroyedOrDisplaced: some remaining force of Reconstruction equality in public life
sourcesOrRationale: National Archives right-to-vote and milestone-document context for post-Reconstruction civil-rights retrenchment.

title: 19th Amendment
summary: The amendment prohibited denying the vote on the basis of sex, widening formal political inclusion even within an unequal order.
destroyedOrDisplaced: male-only national suffrage assumptions
sourcesOrRationale: National Archives: Right to Vote and 19th Amendment milestone materials.

title: Social Security Act
summary: The Social Security Act enlarged federal responsibility for economic security and helped redefine what citizenship could expect from the national state.
destroyedOrDisplaced: the idea that household insecurity should remain mostly private or local
sourcesOrRationale: National Archives milestone documents and Social Security Act materials.

title: Roosevelt's 'Day of Infamy' address and U.S. entry into World War II
summary: The attack on Pearl Harbor and Roosevelt's address marked the full turn into wartime mobilization and global war.
destroyedOrDisplaced: remaining hope of staying outside the global conflict
sourcesOrRationale: National Archives milestone documents: Franklin D. Roosevelt's 'Day of Infamy' Address.

title: Civil Rights Act
summary: The Civil Rights Act used federal power to attack legalized discrimination in public life and employment.
destroyedOrDisplaced: the legal foundation of Jim Crow in major public domains
sourcesOrRationale: National Archives: Civil Rights Act (1964).

title: Voting Rights Act
summary: The Voting Rights Act made federal protection of political inclusion materially enforceable in the face of local suppression.
destroyedOrDisplaced: many local mechanisms of voter suppression
sourcesOrRationale: National Archives right-to-vote and Voting Rights Act materials.

title: United States presidential election of 1968
summary: The 1968 election crystallized backlash, polarization, law-and-order politics, and long-run partisan realignment.
destroyedOrDisplaced: the remaining coherence of the mid-century political settlement
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica: United States presidential election of 1968.

title: Watergate scandal
summary: Watergate deepened distrust in executive power and turned institutional suspicion into a durable feature of public life.
madePossible: stronger investigative and adversarial political culture
destroyedOrDisplaced: some residual confidence in presidential honesty
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica summary: Watergate scandal.

title: September 11 attacks
summary: The September 11 attacks reordered security politics, war, surveillance, and executive power in the United States.
destroyedOrDisplaced: the assumption of strategic distance from catastrophic attack on U.S. soil
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica: September 11 attacks overview.

title: Financial crisis of 2007-08
summary: The collapse of the housing and credit system exposed deep fragility in the economic order and intensified distrust, precarity, and inequality.
madePossible: longer distrust of elites and markets
destroyedOrDisplaced: confidence in the durability of the pre-crash financial settlement
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica Money: financial crisis of 2007–08.

title: United States presidential election of 2016
summary: The 2016 election marked a major break in political norms, media practice, and confidence in established party mediation.
destroyedOrDisplaced: assumptions about stable campaign norms and party gatekeeping
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica: United States presidential election of 2016.

title: Indian Removal Act
summary: The Indian Removal Act authorized the president to negotiate removal of tribes east of the Mississippi, deepening dispossession inside the expanding republic.
madePossible: faster transfer of southeastern land to white settlement
destroyedOrDisplaced: any stable claim that expansion could respect Indigenous sovereignty
sourcesOrRationale: National Park Service Trail of Tears history and Britannica Indian Removal Act overview.

title: PATCO strike and Reagan's firing of air traffic controllers
summary: The crushing of the PATCO strike signaled a harder executive and employer stance toward organised labour and became a defining marker of the neoliberal turn.
destroyedOrDisplaced: expectations that federal labour conflict would end in negotiated accommodation
sourcesOrRationale: Ronald Reagan Presidential Library PATCO strike summary and Britannica Reaganomics overview.

title: COVID-19 pandemic in the United States
summary: The COVID-19 pandemic exposed public-health vulnerability, widened social and economic strain, and accelerated remote, platform-mediated daily life.
consequences: health inequity and institutional distrust intensified
destroyedOrDisplaced: assumptions of late-modern continuity and low public-health vulnerability
sourcesOrRationale: CDC Museum COVID-19 Timeline.

title: Dust Bowl
summary: Severe drought, overcultivation, and wind erosion devastated parts of the Great Plains, driving displacement and exposing the ecological fragility inside the Depression-era order.
madePossible: stronger federal conservation and relief responses
clearer public understanding of environmental vulnerability
destroyedOrDisplaced: assumptions that intensive expansion and cultivation carried little ecological cost
sourcesOrRationale: Library of Congress Dust Bowl primary-source materials and Britannica Dust Bowl overview.
